title: 90-Day Deep Work Habit
description: Build a daily deep-work practice grounded in Cal Newport's Deep Work, ultradian 90-minute focus cycles (Kleitman), and implementation intentions (Gollwitzer, 1999). You will progress from 25-minute focus blocks to fully protected 90-minute deep sessions.
resources: Gollwitzer, P. M. (1999). Implementation intentions. American Psychologist.
Kleitman, N. — Basic rest-activity cycle (BRAC) research.
advice: Schedule the block at a fixed time, write an if-then plan ('If it is 9am, then I open my deep-work doc'), and remove phone from room. Track streaks weekly, not daily.

subGoals.title: Week 1: Establish a 25-min daily block
subGoals.description: Lowest-friction entry point. Build the cue → routine loop before extending duration.

subGoals.title: Day 30: 50-min single block, 5 days/week
subGoals.description: Crossing the half-ultradian threshold. Habit should feel automatic by now (Lally et al., 2010: ~66 days median to automaticity).

subGoals.title: Day 60: Two 50-min blocks per day
subGoals.description: Add a second block with a 90-min recovery gap matching the BRAC cycle.

subGoals.title: Day 90: One 90-min deep block daily
subGoals.description: Full ultradian session. This is the target state — protected, scheduled, undistracted.

tasks.title: Daily deep-work block
tasks.description: Phone in another room. One tab. One doc. Timer running. Newport: 'Clarity about what matters provides clarity about what does not.'

tasks.title: Weekly shutdown review
tasks.description: Newport's shutdown ritual. Review last week's blocks, plan next week's slots, identify what broke focus.

tasks.title: Extend block to 50 minutes
tasks.description: Gradual progressive overload — research on attention training shows linear extension beats jumps.

tasks.title: Add second daily block
tasks.description: Place 90+ minutes after the first block ends (BRAC recovery window).

tasks.title: Reach 90-minute single block
tasks.description: Match the natural ultradian peak. Stop at the timer — overrun erodes recovery.
